ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for dried shrimp bun; closest defensible match is bread roll food product. Dried shrimp filling is not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and bakery products broadly; no specific code exists for filled savoury buns with dried shrimp. Approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried shrimp bun as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dried shrimp bun weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single breakfast or snack item.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Shrimp Bun — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dried Shrimp Bun (cr_id F05020038). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; set to -1. Micronutrients not reported by HPB beyond calcium and iron are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried shrimp bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the closest analogous food: white bread roll / enriched white bread (GI ~70–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The dried shrimp filling does not materially alter the GI of the wheat-flour dough base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 29.3 g carbohydrate in 80 g serving) / 100 ≈ 20.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 655 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; one 80 g bun delivers approximately 524 mg sodium, representing over 23% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to half a bun per occasion, avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli sauce, and account for this item within a strict daily sodium budget of no more than 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) combined with 36.6 g carbohydrate and 11.4 g sugar per 100 g; one 80 g bun delivers approximately 29.3 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~20.5, which is classified as high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to half a bun, pair with a source of protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and prefer wholemeal or high-fibre bun variants where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 655 mg/100 g raises blood pressure risk; total fat at 16 g/100 g with unknown saturated and trans fat fractions from likely shortening or lard in the dough presents additional atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake due to high sodium and likely saturated fat content, choose lower-sodium bread alternatives, and avoid frequent consumption as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 655 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; refined carbohydrates and likely pro-inflammatory saturated fats from shortening may exacerbate systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this item due to high sodium, refined carbohydrate load, and potential trans or saturated fat content from the dough, all of which may worsen inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried Shrimp Bun (虾米包; Pau Udang Kering) is a popular Singaporean savoury baked bread roll filled with a mixture of dried shrimp (hae bee), spring onions, and seasonings. The bun is made from a soft, slightly sweet enriched wheat-flour dough that is baked until golden, yielding a fluffy, tender crumb with a lightly crisp exterior. The filling provides an intensely savoury, umami-rich flavour from the concentrated dried shrimp, balanced by the mild freshness of spring onions. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or morning snack and is widely available at Singaporean bakeries, coffee shops (kopitiams), and hawker stalls. Nutritionally, each 100 g delivers 344 kcal, 13.4 g protein, 16 g fat, 36.6 g carbohydrate, 11.4 g sugar, 3.1 g dietary fibre, 655 mg sodium, 143 mg calcium, and 2.6 mg iron. The high sodium content (655 mg/100 g) is a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The high glycemic index (~70) and sugar content make it unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes. The dried shrimp filling is a concentrated source of sodium and purines. Calcium content (143 mg/100 g) is relatively notable for a bread product, likely contributed by the dried shrimp. This item is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or shellfish allergies.
common_user_queries: What is a dried shrimp bun?
How many calories in a dried shrimp bun?
Can diabetics eat dried shrimp bun?
Is dried shrimp bun high in sodium?
Is dried shrimp bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020038) with a reported confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to data unavailability in the HPB source record: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food as of 2026-04-16. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 13.4, fat_g 16.0, carbohydrate_g 36.6) are non-zero. GI value of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread roll (GI ~70) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for dried shrimp bun was found. Halal status is marked false as dried shrimp buns from commercial bakeries may use lard-based shortening; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.562 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with the analogous estimate from the approved University of Sydney GI Database source.
